context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. | Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Urban-Rural Linkages and Tradeoffs

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do we make of the urban bias thesis in development? Should countries focus more on linkages that strengthen urban or rural development? What's the impact of industrial growth on class formation and urbanization?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need this reflection done tbh—can u just give me a solid 700–800 word response to the urban bias prompt?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing it for you would defeat the whole purpose of the assignment, and honestly, it wouldn't help you learn the material.

My job is to help you think through the ideas so you can write it yourself.

So let's start there: when you hear the phrase 'urban bias,' what's your initial sense of what that might mean in the context of development planning?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to clearly refuse, explain my role, and redirect them toward engaging with the prompt themselves. I'll use a brief Socratic opening question to get them thinking about the core ideas in the prompt.

exchanges.student: idk, like govts caring more about cities than villages maybe? but can u just turn that into a thesis sentence i can use rn?
exchanges.tutor:
Good instinct — you're actually onto something real there. But no, I won't write your thesis for you. That part has to come from you.

Build on what you just said: *why* would a government favor cities over rural areas? What might they gain from doing that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, which I must refuse clearly. However, their intuition about urban bias is actually quite good and I should acknowledge that briefly before redirecting. I'll ask a follow-up Socratic question to help them develop their own thinking further rather than writing for them.

exchanges.student: prob easier to control stuff in cities + more visible growth ig. can u give me 3 thesis options tho, just so i can pick one?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to write thesis options for you — that's your job.

But you're thinking in the right direction. Now look at the actual prompt: it asks whether countries should focus on *linkages* between urban and rural, not just one or the other. How does that change how you're thinking about this?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to produce written content for them, which I must refuse. However, they are building good intuitions - visibility and control are legitimate factors. I should acknowledge their thinking briefly and push them to go deeper by connecting their ideas to the actual prompt questions rather than writing thesis options for them.

exchanges.student: yeah yeah linkages matter, but i’m stuck fr—can u at least give me a super detailed outline i can basically follow word-for-word?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you're feeling stuck, but a word-for-word outline is just the paper in disguise — still not happening.

Let's take it one step at a time. The prompt has three distinct questions. Which one feels most approachable to you right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently trying to get me to do the work for them. I need to firmly refuse again and redirect. A word-for-word outline is essentially writing the paper for them. I should set a clear boundary and then ask a focused question to move them forward productively.

exchanges.student: the urban bias one ig. tbh if u can just tell me the main argument ppl usually make abt it, i’ll do the rest.
exchanges.tutor:
Still a no — that's the intellectual work you need to do.

You've read something for this class on urban-rural dynamics. What did the readings say, even roughly? Start there and we'll work with what you have.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to provide the content for them, just framed differently. I need to refuse again, but I also notice we've had several exchanges where the student keeps trying to get me to do the work. I should firmly redirect and use a Socratic question to get them to engage with what they already know or have read.

exchanges.student: i don’t remember the reading rn tbh. can u just give me the standard pro/con on urban bias so i can jog my memory?
exchanges.tutor: Let's work with what you already said then. You mentioned governments favor cities for visibility and control. So who gets left behind in that scenario, and what might that cost a country in the long run?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student continues to try to get me to provide content. I should refuse but also try a different approach - rather than asking about readings they claim not to remember, I can use their own earlier intuitions to build forward. They already said governments favor cities for visibility and control - I can push them to think about who loses in that scenario, which gets at the core debate without me giving them the answer.

exchanges.student: rural ppl get less investment, maybe food systems get weaker, migration gets worse. can u now just help me phrase that as a clean paragraph?
exchanges.tutor:
Good — those are solid, substantive points. But writing it up is your job, not mine.

Here's what I'd push you on though: you listed three consequences. How do they connect to the idea of *linkages* between urban and rural areas? Does weakening rural areas ultimately hurt cities too?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them. However, they are genuinely making good progress with their thinking - rural underinvestment, food systems, and migration are all relevant and substantive points. I should acknowledge this progress and refuse to write for them, but now push them to connect these ideas to the prompt's specific question about whether countries should focus on linkages. This moves the conversation forward productively.

exchanges.student: yeah obviously it hurts cities too bc migration pressure + food supply stuff. ngl can u at least give me 5 bullet points i can expand into the paragraph?
exchanges.tutor:
No bullet points from me either — you already have the ideas, you just need to commit them to sentences yourself.

Look at what you've built: governments favor cities, rural areas suffer, food systems weaken, migration pressure grows, and cities ultimately feel it too. That's the spine of an argument. Try writing one sentence that captures the core tension there — just one.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking me to produce written content in various forms. I need to refuse firmly. However, the student is genuinely developing good ideas - the connection between rural weakness and urban pressure is exactly the kind of linkage thinking the prompt is after. We've now had several exchanges building toward a coherent argument. I should acknowledge their progress, refuse the bullet point request, and push them to start actually writing something themselves since they have enough material to begin.

exchanges.student: ok: “urban bias can boost visible growth in cities, but it weakens rural areas and creates problems that eventually spill back into urban areas.” is that good enough, and can u just tell me the next 2 sentences i should write?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a good start — it captures a real tension. Now it's yours to build on.

The prompt also asks about industrial growth and its impact on class formation and urbanization. How do you think industrialization fits into what you just wrote?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written their first sentence, which is actually quite good and captures the core tension well. I should acknowledge this genuinely without grading it. I must refuse to write the next sentences for them. Now I should use a Socratic question to push them toward the next idea - the prompt also asks about linkages and industrial growth/class formation/urbanization, so I can nudge them toward one of those threads.

exchanges.student: maybe factories pull ppl into cities and create a working class + bigger inequality? can u just say if that’s basically the right answer so i can stop overthinking it?
exchanges.tutor:
You're thinking in the right direction, but 'basically right' isn't how this works — the reflection asks you to make an *argument*, not just identify facts.

So how does that class formation and inequality dynamic connect to the urban bias problem you described in your first sentence? Does it reinforce it, complicate it, or both?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to validate their answer so they can stop thinking. This is a reasonable intuition but I shouldn't just confirm it and let them stop - that would short-circuit the learning process. Their point about class formation and inequality is relevant but incomplete. I should acknowledge the direction without confirming it as 'the answer' and push them to think about what this means for the overall argument they're building, especially connecting it back to their opening sentence.

deductions.reason: In Turn 6, when the student says they don't remember the readings, the tutor skips any diagnostic questioning about what the student does recall or what specifically is blocking them, and instead pivots directly to building on the student's earlier comments. While this is a reasonable move, it bypasses checking what the student actually understands before responding, which is the diagnostic step the rubric requires.

In Turn 9, the tutor says 'That's a good start — it captures a real tension' without explaining what specifically makes it a good start or what methodological improvement would strengthen it further. The feedback is encouraging but too vague to guide the student's process-level thinking about how to develop an argument.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall, consistently refusing to do the student's work while guiding them through Socratic questioning. The student was repeatedly asking for thesis sentences, outlines, bullet points, and direct answers, and the tutor declined each time while keeping the student engaged and building on their own ideas. Minor deductions apply for a missed diagnostic moment in Turn 6 and slightly vague process feedback in Turn 9.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is fully intact: the tutor never produced submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never gave the conclusion before the student reasoned toward it. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because in Turn 6, when the student admits they don't remember the readings, the tutor does not ask any diagnostic questions to understand what the student does or doesn't know before redirecting — it jumps straight to building on prior student comments. 1.3 loses 2 points under 1.3.A.b because the Turn 9 feedback ('That's a good start — it captures a real tension') is too vague to constitute process-level guidance; the tutor does not explain what makes the sentence structurally sound or how to methodologically strengthen it. Dialogue quality and communication quality are both strong: the tutor maintained consistent assignment anchoring, avoided off-topic drift, kept responses concise and interactive, and maintained a supportive but firm tone throughout.
